questions.question: Write a recursive C function to implement binary search on a sorted integer array. Explain all parameters used in the function.
keyPoints: Function signature with array, target, low, high parameters
Base case when element is found (return index)
Base case when element not found (return -1)
Calculate mid index properly
Compare and recursively call on appropriate half

questions.question: Explain why binary search is more efficient than linear search. What is the time complexity of binary search and why?
keyPoints: Binary search: O(log n), Linear search: O(n)
Each iteration halves the search space
For n=1000, linear needs up to 1000 comparisons, binary needs max 10
Divide and conquer principle explained

questions.question: What are the prerequisites for applying binary search on an array? Can binary search be applied on a linked list? Justify your answer.
keyPoints: Array must be sorted (ascending or descending)
Random access required for O(1) mid calculation
Linked list doesn't support random access efficiently
Time complexity would degrade to O(n) for linked list

questions.question: Trace the binary search algorithm step by step to find element 23 in array [2, 5, 8, 12, 16, 23, 38, 56, 72, 91]. Show all iterations with values of low, high, and mid.
keyPoints: Iteration 1: low=0, high=9, mid=4, arr[4]=16 < 23, go right
Iteration 2: low=5, high=9, mid=7, arr[7]=56 > 23, go left
Iteration 3: low=5, high=6, mid=5, arr[5]=23 == 23, found at index 5
Total 3 iterations needed

questions.question: What is the difference between iterative and recursive binary search? Discuss space complexity of both approaches.
keyPoints: Iterative uses loop, recursive uses function calls
Iterative space: O(1) - no extra stack memory
Recursive space: O(log n) - due to call stack
Both have same time complexity: O(log n)

questions.question: Explain the term 'divide and conquer' in the context of binary search. How does this principle help in efficient searching?
keyPoints: Divide: Split problem into smaller subproblems (halve array)
Conquer: Solve the relevant subproblem (search one half)
Combine: Results combine implicitly through recursion
Reduces problem size exponentially at each step

questions.question: Write the recurrence relation for binary search and solve it to find the time complexity.
keyPoints: T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1) where O(1) is comparison
Using Masters theorem: a=1, b=2, f(n)=O(1)
log_b(a) = log_2(1) = 0
Since f(n) = O(n^0), case 2 applies: T(n) = O(n^0 * log n) = O(log n)

questions.question: Write a complete C program to implement binary search using recursion. The program should: (a) Accept a sorted array from the user (b) Accept the element to search (c) Implement recursive binary search function (d) Display appropriate message if element is found or not found.
keyPoints: Include necessary headers: stdio.h
Define binarySearch function with proper parameters
Implement base cases: found (return index) and not found (return -1)
Calculate mid safely to avoid overflow
Recursive calls with updated low/high
Main function for user input and function calls
Display result with meaningful message
Test with sample input

questions.question: (a) What is the maximum number of comparisons required to find an element in an array of 1024 elements using binary search? (b) If the array contains 1000 elements, what is the worst-case number of comparisons needed? (c) Explain why the formula mid = low + (high - low) / 2 is preferred over mid = (low + high) / 2.
keyPoints: (a) log₂1024 = 10 comparisons maximum
(b) log₂1000 ≈ 9.97, so 10 comparisons maximum
(c) Explain integer overflow scenario for large indices
low + high can exceed INT_MAX causing overflow
The safer formula is low + (high - low) / 2
This ensures mid always stays within array bounds

questions.question: Compare linear search and binary search with respect to: (a) Time complexity (b) Space complexity (c) Data requirement (d) Implementation complexity (e) Suitability for large datasets. Provide a summary table and explain each point with examples.
keyPoints: Time: Linear O(n) vs Binary O(log n)
Space: Both O(1) iteratively, Binary O(log n) recursively
Data: Linear works on any array, Binary requires sorted array
Implementation: Linear is simpler, Binary needs careful index handling
For n=1 million: Linear worst-case = 1M comparisons, Binary = 20 comparisons
Binary is far superior for large sorted datasets

questions.question: Modify the recursive binary search algorithm to handle the following cases: (a) Array sorted in descending order (b) Find the first occurrence of duplicate elements (c) Count the total number of occurrences of an element in a sorted array.
keyPoints: (a) Reverse the comparison conditions for descending order
(b) When found, check if any earlier occurrence exists in left subarray
(c) Find first and last occurrence, count = last - first + 1
Use modified binary search with leftmost/rightmost detection
Provide clear algorithm modifications with code snippets
